coredata.dc:title: Tuning the energy gap of graphene quantum dots functionalized by OH and COOH radicals: First principle study
coredata.dc:creator: Ojeda-Martínez, Miguel
Pérez Martínez, Arturo N.
El Hamdaoui, Jawad
Courel Piedrahita, Maykel
Feddi, El Mustapha
Thirumuruganandham, Saravana P.
Ojeda Martínez, María L.
Cuevas Figueroa, José L.
Velásquez Ordoñez, Celso
Mowbray, Duncan J.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we performed theoretical calculations based on density functional theory for graphene quantum dots considering three different sizes. We consider graphene quantum dots formed by 7, 19, and 37 rings of C atoms in a hexagonal arrangement. The electronic band structure of graphite and graphene were calculated to evaluate the methodology and parameters used during calculations. The modeled graphene quantum dots structures were initially passivated by H atoms, then the H atoms were gradually replaced by OH or COOH radicals to investigate the influence of oxygen on the chemical stability and the energy gap. Based on the electric dipole moment, the replacement positions were selected. The results demonstrate that all the structures are chemically stable and that the energy gap depends on the number of OH or COOH radicals at the edge of the GQDs. H passivation results in energy gaps of 2.83 eV, 1.87 eV and 1.33 eV, decreasing with increasing OH or COOH radical amount. It was found that the energy gap varies non-monotonically as the OH or COOH radicals increase. To better understand the origin of the energy gap and its changes by OH or COOH radicals, we calculated the DOS and evaluated the HOMO and LUMO by Fermi surfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DFT
Graphene
Quantum dots
SIESTA
Energy gap

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by F-PROMEP-39/Rev-04 SEP-23-005 and the Imbabura cluster of Yachay Tech University , which was purchased under contract No. 2017-024 (SIE-UITEY-007-2017) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of: graphite unit cell, the first Brillouin zone and GQDs formed of 7, 19, and 37 C rings.
Electronic band structure for graphite and graphene calculated by DFT.
Electric dipole moment for GQDs with OH and COOH radicals at different positions.
Formation energy as a function of the number of OH or COOH radicals at the edge of GQDs of 7, 19, and 37 C rings.
Energy gap as a function of the number of OH or COOH radicals on the edge of GQDs formed by 7, 19, and 37 C rings.

Density of states of GQDs formed by 7 C rings considering the cases of fully H passivation, with (a) four and (b) eight OH radicals, (c) fully OH passivation and with (d) one, (e) three and (f) six COOH radicals.

Density of states of GQDs formed by 19 C rings considering the cases of total H passivation, with (a) six, (b) twelve and (c) fully OH passivation. In the case of (d) one, (e) four and (f) nine COOH radicals.

Density of states of GQDs formed by 37 C rings considering the cases of fully H passivation, with (a) eight, (b) sixteen OH radicals and (c) fully OH passivation. In the case of COOH (d) is for one, (e) is for six and (f) is for twelve COOH radicals.

Schematic representation of the Highest Occupied and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) of GQDs: fully H passivated, (a) functionalized with four OH radicals, (b) functionalized with eight OH radicals, (c) fully functionalization with OH radicals and with (d) one COOH radicals, (e) three COOH radicals and (f) six COOH radicals.

Schematic representation of the Highest Occupied and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) of GQDs: fully H passivated, (a) functionalized with six OH radicals, (b) functionalized with twelve OH radicals and (c) fully functionalization with OH radicals. Also, (d) is for one, (e) is for four and (f) is for nine COOH radicals.

Schematic representation of the Highest Occupied and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) of GQDs: fully H-passivated, (a) functionalized with eight OH radicals, (b) functionalized with sixteen OH radicals, (c) fully functionalized with OH radicals (d) one COOH radical, (e) six COOH radical and (f) twelve COOH radical.
Bond distances for the atoms conforming graphene quantum dots studied in this paper.
Energy gap of GQD passivated with OH or COOH radicals.

head.ce:title: Tuning the energy gap of graphene quantum dots functionalized by OH and COOH radicals: First principle study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we performed theoretical calculations based on density functional theory for graphene quantum dots considering three different sizes. We consider graphene quantum dots formed by 7, 19, and 37 rings of C atoms in a hexagonal arrangement. The electronic band structure of graphite and graphene were calculated to evaluate the methodology and parameters used during calculations. The modeled graphene quantum dots structures were initially passivated by H atoms, then the H atoms were gradually replaced by OH or COOH radicals to investigate the influence of oxygen on the chemical stability and the energy gap. Based on the electric dipole moment, the replacement positions were selected. The results demonstrate that all the structures are chemically stable and that the energy gap depends on the number of OH or COOH radicals at the edge of the GQDs. H passivation results in energy gaps of 2.83 eV, 1.87 eV and 1.33 eV, decreasing with increasing OH or COOH radical amount. It was found that the energy gap varies non-monotonically as the OH or COOH radicals increase. To better understand the origin of the energy gap and its changes by OH or COOH radicals, we calculated the DOS and evaluated the HOMO and LUMO by Fermi surfaces.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
Currently, there are some years after the discovery of graphene in 2004 when Geim and Novoselov completed the characterization of an isolated graphene sheet [1]. In general, graphene is a 2-dimensional material formed by carbon atoms in a hexagonal arrangement like a honeycomb [1,2]. After its discovery, the scientific community has been very interested in graphene since its properties are different from some other materials, for example, graphene has a high thermal conductivity ranging from 2600 to 5300 W m−1 K−1 when it is suspended [3]. On the other hand, if graphene is placed on a substrate the thermal conductivity is reduced to 370–600 W m−1 K−1, which is smaller than the value for graphene suspended, but comparable with the 237 W m−1 K−1 of aluminum or the 401 W m−1 K−1 of copper [3]. Also, it has a high carrier mobility around 2 × 10
5
cm
2
V−1 s−1 with carrier density of ≈ 2 × 1011 cm−2 for a single layer suspended [4]. Other very interesting properties of graphene are its high elasticity and flexibility, high hardness, and high resistance making it a good candidate for wear-resistence coatings or body armor [5,6], in fact, according to literature graphene is 200 times stronger than steel with a strength as high as 130 GPa [6]. Due to all the promising properties, graphene is considered as the thinnest, most flexible and strongest material known [7]. All of these features open a lot of opportunities for graphene in applications for electronics used as a gas sensor [8], as a material for spintronic devices [9], in quantum informatic science [10], or energy storage [11], also it could be an attractive material for renewable energy, health care, and automotive industry [7,12]. Besides, graphene is considered the basic structure of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, or graphene oxide [13,14]. In special, GQDs are small pieces of graphene whose size is less than 100 nm and have unique electrical, optical, and mechanical properties. In fact, due to the stable fluorescence, the high surface area-to-volume ratio, the low toxicity, and solubility in water, GQDs are very suitable for bioimaging, biological markers, sensors, energy storage, catalysis, and drug delivery [15–18]. Also, GQDs have high electron mobility, meaning electrons can move quickly and efficiently. This makes them useful for applications such as transistors, solar cells [19–22] and in spintronics devices [23,24]. Furthermore, GQDs are highly stable and can be used in a variety of environments, including extreme temperatures and pressures. Following the literature, GQDs are synthesized using a variety of techniques, in addition, the attachment of chemical groups as COOH, OH, CS or CO helps to control the size and shape of the GQD, as well as adding porousity to them, as a result GQDs can be used for a wide variety of applications [12,23,25,26]. The hydrothermal method is one of the most common methods for synthesizing GQDs [15,27,28], the method produces GQDs in a controlled manner with a high degree of optical absorption and photoluminescence. During hydrothermal reactions the GQDs interact with H
2
O and/or H
2
O
2
which are the principal solvents used in this method to create the GQDs. Hence, some of those oxygen or hydrogen atoms may bond at the edges or surfaces of GQDs [29–31]. Oxygen atoms can act as a dopant, increasing the conductivity, or they can act as a passivating agent, reducing the reactivity of the quantum dot. In addition, oxygen atoms can also affect the optical properties of the quantum dot, such as its absorption and emission spectra [32]. The hydrothermal reaction with H
2
O or H
2
O
2
can cause GQDs bond with oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl (OH), carbonyl (CO), and carboxyl (COOH) groups, which can affect the surface chemistry of the GQDs [25]. These functional groups enhance GQD solubility in polar solvents and facilitate their functionalization. In addition, hydrothermal reactions alter the size, shape, and photoluminescence properties of the GQDs.

Taking it into account some research has been done considering different shapes of graphene quantum dots, for example rectangular, triangular or hexagonal. Also, depending on the edge arrangement, it could be armchair or zigzag. The energy gap varies depending on the shape of GQDs, such as 3.68 eV for hexagonal-armchair GQDs, 1.82 eV for triangular GQDs, and 0.71 eV for rectangular GQDs [23,32,33]. Furthermore, Zhao et al. studies conclude that the energy gap values could be tuned by functionalizing the edges with different elements [32]. Abdelsalam et al. reported that the energy gap for hexagonal armchair GQDs decreases when the edge is functionalized with oxygen atoms [33]. To understand the effect at surface or edge functionalization in GQDs different models have been proposed. Some promising applications of GQDs have been identified, such as, the reported by Abdelsalam et al. who has studied GQDs obtained from nanoribbon superlattices to be employed as membranes to absorb heavy metals like Cd, Pb, and Hg [25,26]. Other studies showed that GQDs could be applied to create efficient light-emitting diodes (LEDs) with a wide range of colors, and in the development of GQD-based optoelectronic devices [19,20,27,34]. Guanghan Li et al. studied the design, construction and biomedical application of sensors based on GQDs, according to them, GQDs could be used for biomedical detection, imaging, and monitoring depending on their shape or functional groups.

However, oxygen effects on GQDs are not adequately documented, and the exact mechanisms behind them are not yet fully explored. While some studies have suggested that oxygen can affect GQD properties, the exact nature of this dependency is still unclear [35,36]. In this work, we present a density functional theory investigation of the effect on GQDs when they are functionalized with OH or COOH radicals at the edge. We study the role of these radicals in the chemical stability and the energy gap on the GQDs. All of this to get a better understanding of the effect of the oxygen atoms at the edge on GQDs and develop strategies for controlling those effects.

In Fig. 1, we present the models of H-passivated GQDs used in this work. First, we considered a hexagonal arrangement of carbon atoms forming a graphite unit cell. This consists of two interlocking hexagons, with each carbon atom at the center of a hexagon in different layers. The lattice parameters are a = 2.46 Å and c = 6.7 Å, the separation between layers is half of the lattice parameter c . From the view of the surface of a graphite layer, we could see that one layer is displaced 1.42 Å respect to another layer. After removing one of the staked layers and increasing the value of the lattice parameter c to 12 Å to avoid interaction between replicas, we achieved a perfect graphene unit cell, which was relaxed to the minimum energy before the creation of the GQDs. The supercell used to create the GQDs was based on the supercell scheme. In this case, the relaxed graphene unit cell was reproduced along the lattice parameters a and b until the unit cell was repeated 16 × 16 × 1 times. The modeled GQDs were constructed by removing atoms until 7, 19, and 37 rings of C atoms were conserved, all dangling bonds were passivated with H atoms. Fig. 1 shows the models of GQDs. Furthermore, the H atoms at the edge in the GQDs were replaced by OH radicals and COOH radicals to represent the functionalization of GQDs. The GQDs with OH radicals in our figures are labeled as (a), (b) and (c), while the GQDs with COOH radicals are labeled as (d), (e) and (f). The COOH radicals in GQDs are added until half of the H atoms are reached. This is performed to avoid interaction between two nearby COOH radicals.

SIESTA software [37,38] was used to relax the structures and calculate the energy gap by DFT. We employed the PZ functional, which is based on the Local Density Approximation (LDA), in conjunction with a double zeta basis set plus polarization (DZP) [38,39]. We used a norm-conserving pseudopotential [40] for C ([He]2s
2
2p
2
), H (1s
1
), and O ([He]2s
2
2p
4
) atoms to represent the core and valence electrons, and the transferability was checked by comparing pseudo-atom and all-electron calculations for isolated atoms in the atom package [37]. The mesh cutoff, energy shift, and electronic temperature were set to 150 Ry, 200 meV, and 300 K, respectively. The conjugated gradient method (CG) was applied to get well-converged structures until the forces become less than 0.001 eV/Å with a self-consistency for the density matrix of 10−4 which allow to get accurate results [39]. The Brillouin zone was described by a 40 × 40 × 40 k-point mesh for graphite and a 40 × 40 × 1 k-point mesh for graphene and we only used the gamma point for GQDs.

After relaxation of the unit cell for graphite and graphene, the resulting values for the lattice parameter a and c were 2.472 Å and 6.809 Å for graphite, while in graphene a was 2.474 Å and c remains with a value of 12 Å, this results are in good accordance with the result previously presented in [41]. Also, we calculated the electronic band structure for graphite and graphene, in both cases, the electronic band structure is very similar, the valence and conduction bands are made up of overlapping 2p orbitals from carbon atoms. The valence band is separated from the conduction band by a bandgap of zero energy, located at the high symmetry point K as shown in Fig. 2, it means that electrons can easily move between the two bands, as a result, graphite and graphene are both excellent electrical and thermal conductors [4]. According to our results, the parameters employed during our calculations can reproduce the electronic band structure of graphite and graphene [42,43]. Therefore, those parameters can be used to relax and calculate the energy levels in GQDs.

As a initial step, we relaxed the GQDs passivated only with H atoms as shown in Fig. 1. After the relaxation, we calculated the energy levels, we obtained an energy gap of 2.83 eV, 1.87 eV, and 1.33 eV for GQDs of 7, 19 and 37 C rings, respectively. Subsequently, we replaced the H atoms with OH or COOH radicals. We replaced one H atom at a time in the GQDs considering the electric dipole moment obtained by the replacement of H for OH or COOH, as presented in Fig. 3, which present the analysis for the GQDs of 7 and 19 C rings. The first replacement can be done in any H atom since the formation energy does not differ more than 0.001 eV between positions. The OH radical cause the GQDs to have an agglomeration of negative charges near the oxygen atom due to its electronegativity, while on the opposite side the GQD has a positive charge creating an electrical dipole moment. For the second OH radical, we assumed that the positive charge in the GQD will interact with the negative charge of the O in the OH, causing it to be placed so far away from the first OH, besides, we expected that the second OH would decrease the electric dipole moment in the GQD (Fig. 3(a) and (c)). For the third OH, we consider, as in the first one, that it would be added where the electric dipole moment increases due to the electronegativity of O atom (Fig. 3(b) and (d)). In Fig. 3(c) and (d), we present the electric dipole moment results for different OH positions in the GQD of 19 carbon rings. we observed and conclude that in an even number of OH radicals, the dipolar moment is very low when the OH are placed on opposite sides. In contrast, in an odd number of OH, the electric dipolar moment is maximum in the far position. Based on this consideration, we replace the H atom for OH and calculate the electric dipole moment, the results are presented in Fig. 3(e), we performed the same assumption for the case of COOH radicals the results are in Fig. 3(f). In Fig. 3(e) and (f), we can see a discrepancy for the GQD of 19 C rings, in which an exchange in the electric dipole moment occurs between GQDs with odd and even numbers of OH radicals or COOH radicals due to the odd number of H atoms to replace at each edge.

In order to evaluate the chemical stability of the GQDs, we calculated the formation energy for the GQDs with H passivation and functionalized with OH and COOH radicals, by the next equation
E
f
=
E
0
− ∑
n
i
μ
i
n
T
where
E
0
is the ground state energy of the graphene layer,
n
i
and
μ
i
indicate the occurrences of chemical potentials for C, O and H elements and
n
T
is the total number of atoms per cell [41]. In Fig. 4 we present the values of the formation energy. The negative energy indicate that all the structures proposed are chemically stable.

As we know, formation energy is a measure of the stability of a compound or crystal relative to its constituent elements. It means that lower formation energies indicate greater stability so as we could see in Fig. 4(a), (b), (c), (d) and (f), the most stable configuration is GQD without any radicals. As the number of OH or COOH radicals increases, the chemical stability of the GQDs decreases. Also, we could see in Fig. 4(a), (b) and (c) three zones in each GQDs where we plot the variation of the formation energy with the number of OH radicals (orange dashed line, m is the value of the slope). We found that GQDs with a few OH radicals have the highest variation in the energy of formation, while the lowest variation in the formation energy is for the GQDs almost full with OH radicals. The decrease in the formation energy is presented in Fig. 4(e) and (f) too, it is shown that as the number of COOH radicals increases the stability decreases. In this case, the formation energy showed two zones of variation at different rates (dashed orange line). Fig. 4(d) shows a different behavior since the formation energy decreases as the number of COOH radicals increases. Furthermore, we measured the bond distance between the atoms conforming the GQDs, we found that the CC bond at the surface of the GQDs has a value of 0.143 nm, but this value is modified to a lower value of 0.139 nm for the CC nearest of an OH or COOH radical, this effect could be attributed by the Coulomb interaction produced by the O atom resulting in a decrease of the bond length. Table 1 presents the values obtained and compares them to literature values.
As a next step, we calculated the energy gap as a function of the number of OH or COOH radicals added at the edge of the GQDs. The resulting values of the energy gap are presented in Fig. 5.

We could see that in all cases the addition of OH or COOH radicals decreases the energy gap of GQDs. In Fig. 5, we could observe that the energy gap depends on the OH or COOH radicals present at the edge of the GQD, and we can also observe in Fig. 5 that the energy gap for GQDs varies as the number of those radicals increases. Respectively, the maximum and minimum values are 2.83 and 2.13 eV for (a), 1.87 and 1.44 eV for (b), and 1.33 and 1.00 eV for (c), while the maximum and minimum values are 2.83 and 2.30 eV for (d), 1.87 and 1.55 eV for (e), and 1.33 and 1.06 eV for (f). The energy gap values for the GQDs marked by the dashed lines in Fig. 5 are listed in Table 2. Abdelsalam et al. has been reported the energy gap of zigzag termination GQD as 2.89 eV [23], Zhao et al. used the HSE functional to calculate the energy gap in GQDs considering different sizes and passivation, in his work the energy gap obtained for an H passivated GQD of 11 C rings is 1.5 eV, 1.38 eV for an GQD with two OH radicals, 1.25 eV for six OH radicals and 1.43 for two COOH radicals [32].

Also, we could see from Fig. 5 that the energy gap variation is non-monotone. To understand the non-monotone variation of the energy gap, we calculated the density of states (DOS) for the GQDs fully passivated with H atoms and OH or COOH radicals. Also, we calculated the DOS in GQDs for OH and COOH radicals replacing the H passivation as by the dashed lines in Fig. 5.

In Fig. 6 we presented the DOS for GQDs considering the cases of fully H passivation, with four (a) and eight (b) OH radicals and fully OH passivation (c). In the case of COOH, Fig. 6(d), (e) and (f) present the DOS for 1, 3 and 6 COOH radicals.

The Fermi energy was set to 0 eV to get a better understanding of the location of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). From the fully passivated model, we can see that the C atoms contribute the most to the DOS. Adding OH or COOH radicals to the GQDs leads to some O states near Fermi energy levels. As expected, with the increment of those radicals, distinct energy levels appear in the HOMO and LUMO, as a result, the energy gap decreases. Fig. 6 shows that the DOS has a higher contribution of O atoms for energy levels below Fermi energy than for energy levels above Fermi energy.

When a functional group interacts with the carbon atoms in graphene or GQDs, it can distort the lattice structure and disrupt the π -electron delocalization. During this disruption, localized electronic states are formed near the OH or COOH, which can act as electron traps and affect electronic properties. This effect is particularly pronounced in the valence band of graphene, which consists of two degenerate π -bands formed by the overlap of the p-orbitals of the carbon atoms. Fig. 6 shows that the DOS is more strongly affected by introducing OH or COOH radicals for energy levels below the Fermi energy than for levels above it. This suggests that the disruption of the π -electron system in the valence band is more significant than in the conduction band. The π -electron delocalization in graphene is responsible for many unique properties, such as high electrical conductivity, electron mobility, and optical transparency [50].

Also in Fig. 7, we presented the DOS for GQDs considering the cases of total H passivation, with (a) six, (b) twelve OH radicals and (c) fully OH passivation. For COOH radicals in Fig. 6 we present the DOS for (d) one, (e) four and (f) nine COOH radicals.

Similarly to Fig. 6, the DOS of GQD full H passivated is formed mainly by the interaction of C atoms. However, as the number of OH or COOH radicals increases, oxygen atoms add new levels near HOMO and LUMO, leading to a decrease in the energy gap. Also in Fig. 7, we could observe that there are more energy levels produced by O in the HOMO than in the LUMO.

In Fig. 8 we present the DOS for GQDs considering the cases of fully H passivation, with eight and sixteen OH radicals and fully OH passivation. In the case of COOH (d) is for four, (e) is for eight and (f) is for sixteen COOH radicals. The DOS exhibits the same trend, in the O atoms contribute to an increase in energy levels near the HOMO and LUMO, thereby reducing energy gaps. In Fig. 8, we can see clearly that the O atom contribute poorly to the HOMO and LUMO.

As a result of the DOS, we consider that functional groups with oxygen can act as electron acceptors. This results in new energy levels near the HOMO increasing its energy which reduces the energy gap. On the other hand, they can also act as electron donors [31]. This can create new energy levels in the LUMO and decrease its energy and reduce the energy gap too. Nevertheless, the DOS does not clearly explain the non-monotone variation in the energy gap, but from the DOS we could see that around the Fermi energy the HOMO and LUMO split.

To explain, in Fig. 9, we presented the HOMO and LUMO for the GQDs considering the cases totally H-passivated, (a) functionalized with four OH radicals, (b) functionalized with eight OH radicals and (c) completely functionalized with OH radicals. As we could see, in the case of GQDs fully H-passivated the main contributors to HOMO and LUMO are the outermost C atoms. Due to the increase in the number of OH radicals, the central C atoms on the GQDs contribute to energy states that produce HOMO and LUMO. In the case of GQDs fully functionalized with OH radicals the main contributions for HOMO and LUMO return to the outermost C atoms along with the interaction with the O at the edge. Considering the GQDs with COOH, Fig. 9(d), (e) and (f) show the HOMO and LUMO for 1, 3 and 6 COOH radicals. Furthermore, HOMO and LUMO for a GQD of 7 C rings have contributions from all C atoms, but with an increase in COOH radicals, the central atoms diminish their contribution until the outermost C atoms produce most of the energy levels.

In Fig. 10 we presented the HOMO and LUMO for GQDs of 19 C rings considering the cases of fully H-passivation, (a) functionalized with six OH radicals, (b) functionalized with twelve OH radicals and (c) complete functionalized with OH radicals. For fully H-passivated GQDs, the HOMO and LUMO are mainly determined by the C atoms at the edge of the GQD. Also, the addition of the OH radicals modified the atoms that contribute to the HOMO and LUMO from the outside to the central C atoms in the GQDs. Furthermore, when the GQDs are fully passivated with OH radicals, HOMO and LUMO states are formed for the C atoms at the edge, as well as their interactions with oxygen atoms. I the case of GQDs with COOH, Fig. 10(d), (e) and (f) present the HOMO and LUMO for 1, 4 and 9 COOH radicals. As in the GQD of 7 C rings with COOH, HOMO and LUMO for the GQD of 19 C rings have contributions from almost all C atoms when few COOH are added. A rise in COOH radicals dims the central atoms contribution to the energy level until most are produced by the outermost C atoms.

Finally, in Fig. 11 we presented the HOMO and LUMO for the GQDs of 37 C rings considering the cases of fully H-passivated, (a) functionalized with eight OH radicals, (b) functionalized with sixteen OH radicals and (c) complete functionalized with OH radicals. We found that the HOMO and LUMO behavior are similar to the previous GQDs. On fully H and OH or COOH passivations, HOMO and LUMO are produced by the C atoms at the edge, however, as the number of OH varies, the central atoms play a greater role in the generation of electronic states for the HOMO and LUMO.

As we could see, for the GQDs the HOMO and LUMO are modified due to the redistribution of the energy levels from the C atoms when the OH radicals increase. We suppose that as a result of the addition of the OH radicals, the O atoms had an electrostatic interaction with the electrons of the carbon atoms in the center of the GQDs. Due to the high electronegativity of the O atoms, electrons are attracted to the central C atoms of the GQDs. This results in the apparition of some energy levels as we could see in the DOS.

In this work, we study the effect of OH and COOH radicals on the formation energy and energy gap for GQDs. We modeled three GQDs formed by 7, 19, and 37 rings of C atoms in a hexagonal form. The GQDs were passivated with H atoms, and then we replaced them one by one to get full OH or half COOH passivation in the GQDs. We observed that all the GQDs were chemically stable. However, as the number of OH or COOH radicals increases the formation energy increases, and we also found that the formation energy has a higher variation for a few OH radicals than when it is almost fully passivated. An exception occurs for the case of 7 C rings GQD, where the formation energy decreases with increasing COOH radicals. Furthermore, we found that GQDs with OH radicals have an energy gap variation from the maximum value to the minimum as 2.83 to 2.13 eV for (a), 1.87 to 1.44 eV for (b), and 1.33 to 1.00 eV for (c), while in the case of COOH passivation the maximum and minimum values are 2.83 and 2.30 eV for (d), 1.87 and 1.55 eV for (e), and 1.33 and 1.06 eV for (f). As a result of interactions with oxygen, the energy gap variation is not monotone due to the incorporation of various atoms into the energy levels. To demonstrate the non-monotone variation of the energy gap caused by oxygen, we presented the DOS and HOMO, and LUMO by Fermi surfaces. We conclude that the non-monotone variation of the energy gap could be caused by the redistribution of the energy levels from the C atoms when the OH or COOH radicals increase.

Miguel Ojeda-Martínez: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration. Arturo N. Pérez Martínez: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Jawad El Hamdaoui: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Maykel Courel Piedrahita: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Supervision. El Mustapha Feddi: Methodology, Software, Investigation. Saravana P. Thirumuruganandham: Resources, Visualization, Conceptualization. María L. Ojeda Martínez: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. José L. Cuevas Figueroa: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Supervision. Celso Velásquez Ordoñez: Visualization, Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Duncan J. Mowbray: Resources, Visualization, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
